question: tell me how to check if my timing is correct
content:
What “timing” really means in bowling
Timing is not about how your steps look early in the approach.
Timing is the relationship between your body and the swing at the foul line.
In a properly timed shot:
Your body arrives first
Your swing finishes after
This order is critical. It allows gravity to control the swing, keeps the release clean, and lets power transfer efficiently into the ball without muscle.
We could say, good timing means your body stops before the ball does.

The only reliable timing checkpoint
Timing can be judged accurately at one moment only:
When your sliding foot finishes at the foul line.
At that exact instant, three things must happen together:
1.	Your slide foot is flat and stable on the floor
This shows your body has finished moving forward and is balanced.
2.	Your body is already stopped and under control
No drifting, no rushing, no falling forward.
3.	The ball is still parallel (or very close to parallel) to the floor
The swing is still moving freely and has not been forced or rushed.
When these three align, timing is correct.

Why timing must be checked at the foul line
Before the foul line:
The body is still moving
Speed can be adjusted
The brain can compensate
Muscle can hide problems
At the foul line:
The body stops
Balance is locked in
Compensation is no longer possible
This makes the foul line the only objective moment where timing tells the truth.
Looking at step 1, 2, or 3 is unreliable because:
Different swing heights change arrival time
Faster or slower feet can mask errors
Two bowlers can look “synced” early and still have opposite timing problems

What correct timing feels like
With correct timing:
The swing feels free, not controlled
The release feels effortless
Speed is consistent without forcing
Balance at the line feels stable and calm
The ball comes off the hand naturally
Many bowlers describe it as:
“The ball just shows up at the right moment.”

What incorrect timing looks like
Early timing (most common)
The ball arrives too soon.
At the foul line:
The ball is already dropping or past parallel
The body is still moving or trying to catch up
This forces the bowler to:
Pull the arm
Grab the ball
Slow the swing artificially
Lose balance and revs
Early timing creates inconsistency and fatigue.

Late timing
The body arrives too soon.
At the foul line:
The slide foot is flat
The ball is still high behind the body
This often leads to:
Extra effort at the release
Lofting
Inconsistent speed control

How to check timing correctly (practical method)
The most accurate way to check timing is video from the side.
1.	Record from a side angle, perpendicular to the lane
2.	Play the video frame by frame
3.	Pause exactly when the slide foot becomes flat at the foul line
4.	Observe the ball position at that same frame
Ball near parallel → timing is correct
Ball already dropping → early timing
Ball still high → late timing
This removes guesswork. Feelings can lie, video does not.

The universal timing rule (memorize this)
If your slide foot is flat and stable while the ball is still parallel, your timing is correct.
This rule applies to:
One handed and two handed bowlers
Any number of steps
Any tempo or style
Everything else in the approach is just a method to arrive at that moment correctly.

Why timing matters more than almost anything else
Correct timing:
Improves balance automatically
Increases revs without effort
Stabilizes speed
Cleans up the release
Reduces physical strain
Makes shots repeatable under pressure
Most technical problems in bowling are timing problems in disguise.
Fix the timing relationship at the foul line, and many other issues disappear without direct correction.
